topic: Someone receives a friend request from a childhood bully
raw_output:
[story 1]

My phone buzzed on the kitchen counter, the screen illuminating my face in the pre-dawn gloom. A notification popped up: "Mark R. has sent you a friend request." My breath hitched. I froze, the coffee mug halfway to my lips, the warmth suddenly feeling alien against my skin. Mark. Mark Peterson. The name felt like a physical blow. My fingers, clumsy and unresponsive, hovered over the screen. A slow, creeping dread began to coil in my stomach, tightening until I felt like I couldn't draw a full breath. I stared at the profile picture—a grainy, overly confident grin I hadn't seen in fifteen years—and my jaw went slack. I silently shoved the phone face-down on the counter, my heart hammering a frantic rhythm against my ribs.

[story 2]

I was scrolling through old photos, trying to find a distraction, when the notification arrived. A friend request. My eyes scanned the screen twice, then a third time, as if hoping the message would vanish. It was from Alex. Alex Vance. The name resonated with a sudden, cold jolt deep in my chest. I dropped the remote control. It clattered loudly on the hardwood floor, the sound echoing in the sudden, heavy silence of my apartment. My hands started trembling, a subtle, uncontrollable vibration running up my arms. I squeezed my eyes shut for a moment, trying to steady the sudden rush of adrenaline flooding my system, before forcing myself to look again at the screen, the image of that familiar, unwelcome face burning behind my eyelids.

[story 3]

The chime pulled me from a deep concentration on my laptop. It was a connection request. I blinked, rereading the name: 'Ryan T.' My entire body went rigid. I leaned back in my chair, the cheap plastic groaning under my weight, and stared blankly at the monitor. A strange, hollow sensation washed over me, like someone had suddenly muted the world. I didn't move for a full minute, just absorbing the reality of the digital intrusion. My palms grew slick with perspiration. I slowly reached for the phone, my movements deliberate and slow, as if navigating a minefield. When I finally tapped the notification, the ensuing silence in the room felt deafening, amplifying the sudden, sharp awareness of everything that had just been reopened.
